data.title: Dollar General Corp: Still Attractive At This Valuation
data.description:
Summary

I gave a buy rating to Dollar General Corp (DG) in September ’25, with my key thesis being that DG’s fundamentals have improved; there has been clear momentum in demand, and the new digital levers are working. The “trade-in” dynamic from higher-income shoppers is getting more apparent, and DG showed us that it can continue to capture discretionary purchase demand. Combined with confidence from management (via their revised guide), I believe the stock still looks attractive at this valuation.

The Trade-In Thesis

The key debate I had for DG was that the increasing mix of higher-income shoppers was becoming a trend. To my surprise, this was just a trend; it is an accelerating trend, which makes me think this could really become a structural tailwind. Just for reference, DG managed to inch out a 2.5% y/y increase in customer traffic despite the entire retail sector facing consumer spending weakness. And importantly, it was confirmed that the traffic increase came from all income cohorts (low, middle, and high).

The most important data point is that households earning >$100,000 were the fastest-growing customer cohort in the latest quarter. I have given my thoughts on this higher-income “trade-in” impact before, and I believe the framing shouldn’t just be that the consumers are looking to save costs (i.e., looking for value purchases). Surely there is a group of high-income customers that are shifting to DG because of that reason, but I believe there is a group that switched to DG because they want to. Now, this is an entirely different framing because this type of demand is stickier and suggests that DG is successfully expanding its total addressable market. The way I see it, when a six-figure earner is not just buying essentials but also buying from discretionary categories (can be seen by the 7.6% comp in the 'Value Valley' segment, more on this below), it proves that DG is meeting a certain standard of preference, not just necessity.

The Value Valley Proves Discretionary Demand Isn't Dead

The fact that DG’s The Value Valley assortment delivered a 7.6% comp tells us that discretionary spending is not entirely dead. Consumers still want discretionary products; they are only more focused on price. You could argue that because The Value Valley prices its products at $1, hence, there is demand. I would push back to say that pricing is only part of the equation. It is DG’s execution that made this work. If the product assortment didn't resonate with the consumer's current taste and needs, it doesn’t matter what prices DG sets; the products wouldn’t sell. Within this aspect, there is “having the right product assortment” and “having the right experience.” The former is important, but the latter is what makes DGs different.

It all anchors on the "treasure hunt" dynamic, which I think is the key to purchase conversion because consumers believe they have “discovered” a high-value item for a low price. And this strategy really only works when the perceived risk is low. When you ask a consumer to pay $20 for a home décor item, the reaction is, "Do I really need this?" But at $1, that question is unlikely to pop up in the mind because even if it turns out to be a "bad" purchase, it doesn’t really hurt the wallet. This opens up the shopping occasion, what I call “experimental shopping,” wherein consumers simply buy it and figure out later whether it’s worth it. That effectively solves the hurdle in purchase conversion today, where consumers are leaning towards saying "no" to buying discretionary products (because they want to save money).

Guidance Confirms the Turnaround Is Real

Perhaps the most bullish signal is that management has raised FY25 guidance with confidence. The EPS guide was lifted to $6.3 to $6.50, and same-store sales [SSS] growth to 2.5% to 2.7%, from $5.8 to $6.3, and 2.1% to 2.6% previously. This is a very aggressive upwards revision, which tells me that management is seeing momentum continue into Q4. Note that the guide was upgraded on 4th December, and the DG fiscal quarter ends on 31st October, which means management already has ~5 weeks of Q4 data. For them to raise the guidance, it must at least show that demand has not weakened. 3rd party tracking data also points to acceleration through Q4, which gives comfort.

This upgrade also shifts the stock sentiment because for the last two years, the stock’s narrative has been mostly about negative earnings revisions. The consensus that followed management earnings got disappointed. But with this upgrade, and if DG really meets that guide, the market should start to give more credit to management’s guidance, which will be helpful to anchor near-term expectations (i.e., there will be less surprise/disappointment that could swing the share price).

Valuation

Valuation-wise, DG now trades at 20x forward earnings, which is where I believe it should at least trade at. I still think there is room for multiples to go higher if earnings growth can accelerate to the low teens. The FY25 guide already implies adj. EPS to grow ~10%, as a reminder. But even if multiples don’t expand, a retailer that is doing very well despite the retail environment, expanding its targeted customer spectrum, and having a solid strategy to capture even discretionary purchases can grow adj. EPS at 10% and should continue to trade at 20x (similar to Dollar Tree (DLTR)’s current valuation and earnings growth expectations). Compounding FY24 adj. EPS of $5.92 by 10% over the next three years equates to ~$7.9 adj. EPS. 20x that equates to a share price of ~$158.

Investment Risk

One nuance that I see as a mini red flag is that management guided for new store returns of 16% to 17%, a drop from the historical >20% standard. While 16% to 17% is still attractive, this does suggest that growth via new stores is seeing diminishing returns. If the pace of decline accelerates, the DG growth runway could be shorter than expected.

Conclusion

I maintain a positive view on DG because the improved fundamentals have gotten clearer, showing the expansion of the customer base, proving discretionary demand still exists, and also improved guidance. While store-level returns bear monitoring, the current earnings trajectory still looks healthy.

data.title: Fiserv: Accumulating Through A Transition, Not Timing A Bottom
data.description:
The fall in Fiserv (FISV) after a dismal Q3 2025 guidance opens up more than a speculative window to initiate fresh positions. I feel the current levels are more about market momentum on the downside that could drag share prices below what fundamentals indicate. This fall also appears to be around a credibility reset (with management changes also hampering sentiment).

But this is not a broken business. Valuations today, as my DCF analysis indicates, are pricing in far too much pessimism, like low single-digit growth forever at permanently impaired margins. The real case is that of a potential margin trough, not a permanent regime change. Cash flow should remain intact, and EPS growth should resume after a reinvestment phase.

My Reading of the Corrections

First of all, Fiserv's offering is not a cyclical fintech product. Its payments and transactions infrastructure products are mission-critical systems with high switching costs. The business reaps the repeatability of client relationships and a recurring revenue and ends up with strong free cash flow over a cycle. Such businesses are rarely broken unless the core franchise is impaired.

That said, corrections were legitimate and attributable to three key overlapping shocks that stand out - although the extent of damage appears overdone. Argentina normalization hit growth optics after strong margin and growth trends in 2023-24. Once rates normalized, comparables became tougher, and growth and margin trends suffered. This is not deterioration of the business - only a slowdown of a significant tailwind.

The second factor is confidence altering, in addition to the mechanical shock by itself. The prior 10–12% organic growth guidance was reported to be based on unrealistic assumptions around non-Argentina business growth, aggressive sales productivity and deal conversions, and an extrapolation of peak pricing and revenue levers that drove much of the growth in 2023-2024.

Thirdly, signs of over-earning emerged as some margins were supported by deferred investments and some growth was driven by tactical pushes that do not lend themselves to growth sustainability in the long term. However, Fiserv has now chosen to reinvest in technology and service and shown a willingness to front-load some short term earnings pains to rebuild durable growth in the longer haul.

My reading of the transition strategy for Fiserv is that even if a turnaround in numbers is not likely in the next quarter or two, the bar has been set lower, which should manage investor expectations far better. Core assets still show healthy signs. Clover GPV still grew double digits (excluding gateway conversion noise). Issuing continues to gain share, debit volumes are healthy, and embedded finance platforms are expanding TAM. Importantly, there has been no material client attrition reported or structural damage (like, say, tech obsolescence). Repurchases have continued (7m shares in Q3 with 49m shares still left pre-authorized if the shares dip further). The free cash flow outlook has also not been altered, which limits long-term and materially deeper drawdowns from here on.

And then there's no change in the free cash flow generation capabilities of the company. And if it's run right for the long term, the conversion stays very, very attractive. There is absolutely no change in our capital management plan.

A Valuation Check

For Fiserv, the aim of my DCF is not precision. I simply want to show that under new growth and cash flow assumptions, it is pessimistically priced. According to guidance now, the free cash flow for the full year is expected to be $4.25b in a heavy investment year. That is my starting point for the DCF model. This is a step down from current TTM levels by around 10% and already assumes higher capex, lower margins (has already started to impact the FCF trajectory in 2025). Guidance obviously also already accounts for no tailwinds from Argentina.

According to management's revised conservative guidance, growth in 2026 is likely to be low single digit, before it reverts to mid-single digit growth beyond 2026-27. I assume similar growth in free cash flow, given Fiserv's history of efficient cash conversion. Importantly, this assumption does not rely on margin expansion or cost efficiencies and is, in fact, in line with management's resilient free cash flow generation outlook.

Finally, the DCF uses a WACC of 9.5% for a mature fintech infrastructure player with high recurring revenue, but acknowledges some execution risk. A terminal growth rate of 3% is assumed. The fair value based on these revised and relatively conservative assumptions shows a fair value of above $75 for Fiserv, which is the basis of my overshot fundamentals claim. DCFs are generally assumption-heavy, and changing the terminal rate to 2.5% shows a fair value closer to today's prices. However, as stated at the start of the analysis, I aim to show how well-supported current prices are and most of the pessimism is well baked in, if not overdone.

Patient Buy Opportunity

My DCF assumptions are in line with analyst expectations reported in Seeking Alpha. These revisions have now been downgraded after the Q3 guidance and will await fresh data and developments over the next couple of earnings calls to reward or punish the stock further.

In the upcoming quarterly results and commentary, I will watch out for free cash flow stability even as Fiserv goes through a capex cycle. This is more critical in my view than margin troughs, which are expected (although surprises could still open up further downside). On the business health, continued Clover GPV growth and client retention signals should help stabilize worries.

However, as my analysis finds, current prices are reasonable to start accumulating, given current information. If new information surfaces in Q4 2025 or Q1 2026, the thesis will be revised - particularly if free cash flow conversion shows structural deterioration. If not, any further drawdown should be used to accumulate through the next couple of quarters. Investors have to recognize that expected returns on this position are more likely to show up in 2027 and beyond - this is not a Q4 '25 or Q1 '26 trade. Any surprises relative to what may prove to be conservative guidance, could expedite rerating.

Risks to the Thesis

The biggest risk to the thesis, as I have outlined earlier, is pressure on FCF that is assumed to stay normalized, particularly as Fiserv is entering a reinvestment phase. If elevated capex adds to structural changes to operating costs, it could hamper assumptions of the baseline FCF expectations close to current levels (if not subsequent growth), making the trough more structural than temporary.

Execution risks should also be acknowledged. Any delay in product rollout or service quality issues through transition could limit growth to low single-digit levels. In such a case, the reversion to mid-single-digit growth assumed in my DCF becomes optimistic.

Thirdly, margin recovery may take longer than expected if the transition plays out over years in terms of higher investments - although my DCF model does not rely on margin expansion, it needs margin stability. Sentiment and multiple risks also may remain a drag on the share till credibility (through execution) resurfaces.

data.title: Novo Nordisk: Soaring Demand Met With Production Hiccup (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction and Investment Risk

I am downgrading Novo Nordisk A/S (NVO) from my previous buy rating to a hold rating because Novo Nordisk’s manufacturing plant has been hit by FDA action, which means production of GLP-1 might be delayed. Although the market outlook is appealing, driven by a projected 47% global diabetes growth rate between 2024 and 2050, the FDA action poses a risk that warrants a cautious approach here. I agree Novo Nordisk is strategically positioned because it already controls 68% market share of diabetes medication, making it a dominant player in the market, but this current tide can’t be overlooked.

In the recently released first 9 months of 2025 report, the sales from international operations (IO) grew by around 16% at constant exchange rates. Notably, it is US sales that lead in terms of GLP-1 diabetes and obesity care, resulting in an overall sales growth of 15%. It is estimated that about 810 million people could be living with diabetes in 2050, and around 75 of these diabetic patients could be prescribed GLP-1. Still, the US is leading by around 20% of the overall prescribed GLP-1.

However, so far, there are about 934 million people with obesity prevalence, and only a handful of 4 million people are currently accessing branded AOMs. This gives an extent of market readiness of GLP-1 medications, but stringent FDA approvals take time, which must be met.

So, when Novo Nordisk faces a hiccup like inadequate investigations on contamination and foreign matter in sterile drug products from the FDA, it raises production limitations. This indicates that the company is facing a production slowdown, especially in ready markets such as the US. It is in the US market where it has recently started commercializing its oral weight loss GLP-1 Wegovy to a potential 100 million Americans living with obesity. I think production limitations will also limit upside momentum from limited sales falling short of the demand.

Soaring Demand

In my previous article, I was bullish about the soaring demand for GLP-1. Still, the market has not performed as expected, resulting in a loss of around -22.53% in about 6 months since my previous article. The company faces a slowed demand for its blockbuster drugs (Wegovy and Ozempic weight loss drugs) in 2025. Also, following the expiration of the semaglutide patent in 2026, especially in India and China, the risks of generic drug makers’ entry into the market are present. As such, the company cut its FY2025 sales forecast from 8-14% to 8-11%, resulting in -22.53%. I will expound on this further later in the financial explanations below.

Despite this slowed demand, the market tailwind still exists, given that less than 1% of obese people have so far accessed branded AOMs globally. The GLP treatments for obesity and diabetes

This increasing prevalence is typically coming from adults aged 25 years and above, who will account for about 60% of the global population with diabetes by 2050. Children are still on the lower side, between 5 years and 25 years, who account for 31% of the global population with diabetes. This means that the market is already up for capture.

Now that around 12% of Americans have reported using GLP-1 drugs to manage their weight loss, this is around 42 million Americans. With Wegovy pills’ first market entry as a weight loss medicine in the US, this is likely to boost NVO’s sales in 2026 after a challenging end of 2025. Before facing intense competition from Eli Lily’s GLP-1 pill, which is scheduled to be released later in 2026, NVO has a strong market entry potential.

However, I think the above optimism faces a cautious look from the FDA, driven by the production hiccup at its Indiana manufacturing facility. The concern here is that with the intense competition from Eli Lily, which is also almost launching its GLP-1 pill soon, NVO’s mass production of Wegovy injections would mean less production cost. The flagging by the FDA delays volume manufacturing, which could give its competitor Eli Lily a chance to catch up with its market access.

The reason I am cautious about a production hiccup is that, despite the North Carolina facility maintaining an entire supply chain of the Wegovy pill within the US, it is still a new drug in the market, and I think there is a need to check its market entry acceptance. The Indiana manufacturing plant was scheduled for production of Wegovy injection pens; its flagging means around 20,000 Americans’ prescriptions per week face a possibility of a shortage of Wegovy injection supply. Therefore, if the pill receives market acceptance since it offers the same purpose as injections, there is a likelihood of Wegovy injection customers considering pills. Looking at this through the lens of price, customers with insurance are likely to receive Wegovy pills at $25 less per month. Compared to those without insurance, the price of a monthly dosage goes for around $149 to $299. On the other hand, weekly Wegovy injections cost upwards of $300 per month without insurance. These two possibilities, new drug entry into the market and possible price consideration following the Wegovy production hiccup, present two possibilities that inform my cautious outlook.

Financials and Valuation

As promised, I highlighted that the sales forecast cut due to reduced demand for its GLP-1 resulted in a 20% downward stock price. Now, the company has taken measures such as restructuring with an aim of saving around $1.25 billion per year to improve its financials despite this short-term hiccup. Right now, its revenue is trailing at $49.63 billion, representing 16.64% YoY growth. This is lower than 25% revenue growth in 2024 and 31.2% in 2023, a sign of weakening revenue momentum at a time uncertainty is mounting. This further explains my hold rating.

The company has not yet released its Q4’2025, which is scheduled for early February 2026, but analysts’ revenue estimates for the quarter are $12.07 billion, representing 1% YoY growth. This is likely to result in a total FY2025 revenue estimate of about $48 billion, which is about 18.4% YoY growth. Interestingly, this company has been beating its estimates consistently over the last couple of years. With this precedent, it is possible that it could beat its 2025 projections. However, I anticipate a miss next year following the current headwinds or a significant downward revision in its 2026 projections compared to 2025.

While I expect 2025 to be strong with a possible revenue beat, the joy could be short-lived because in 2026, the revenue faces a possible slump of about -1.28%. This is driven by possible low demand for its GLP-1 medicines due to generic drug production, especially now that India, Brazil, Canada, and China patents are ending in 2026. This includes a reason why I am cautious.

The bottom-line performance has also been impressive YoY, and I don't think 2025 will be an exception. Over the last five years, the company has maintained a consistent positive growth figure in its annual net income, a sign of its strong profit momentum and strong execution. However, the production flagging and expiration of patents has resulted in a -5.14% EPS projection for 2026, which supports my skeptical view of the year. With this possible decline, I do not see a solid upside potential in 2026, which is why I believe there is a reason to be cautious.

I will now wrap up this section by valuing Novo Nordisk using the P/S multiple, comparing it to that of its close competitor Eli Lilly and Company (LLY). The P/S multiple of NVO is currently at 4.87x compared to LLY, which is at 15.75x. My interpretation of that Novo Nordisk’s P/S multiple is that it is fairly valued by the market because its current headwinds discussed above illustrate its stalled P/S multiple below 5x.

Currently, I do not believe Novo Nordisk is undervalued because it is at 4.87x due to its projected revenue decline. The market optimism I am seeing, which Novo Nordisk has the potential to tap into, is demonstrated by LLY’s high P/S ratio of 15.75x, reflecting a high return rate. For instance, while NVO's revenue growth rate for FY2026 is -0.87% YoY according to consensus estimates, LLY's revenue growth for FY2026 is 20.73% YoY. This shows that as NVO’s projected revenue faces a possible dip, LLY’s revenue is facing a double-digit growth, thus justifying its high valuation.

Investment Risk: Looming Patent Expirations

The Looming patent expiration in 2026 on Novo Nordisk’s blockbuster GLP-1 drugs (Wegovy and Ozempic) exposes NVO to a significant sales decline. The regions where it is expiring, such as China and India, represent a vast demographic with rising obesity and diabetes prevalence, which presents a critical setting for long-term growth. Now that generics are about to enter these markets, the possible price of Ozempic and Wegovy downward revision by 65% means possible compressed revenues. Ordinarily, varied research findings show that market share shrinks as generic drugs increase in the market. In India, generic GLP-1 medicines are around 30% to 80% cheaper than their original branded counterparts. This is because the original medication requires capital-intensive research before it is approved and patent restrictions.

Conclusion

There is GLP-1 optimism in the external market, which is why I believe Novo Nordisk stands a chance to rebound, but due to production hiccups and the expiration of patents in 2026, I am cautious here. This is a significant risk that I think warrants a hold rating due to a substantial revenue decline that might be experienced.

data.title: Seanergy Maritime: Cheap At Current Rates, Upgrade To Strong Buy
data.description:
Seanergy Maritime Holdings Corp. (SHIP) is a pure play on the Capesize sector in dry bulk. I have covered the stock before, but given recent changes, along with Q3 2025 earnings being released, I judged it is time to give the company a fresh review.

It is my belief that a valuation of $200 million is undervalued and the market is underestimating the sector. The threat to Chinese iron demand is real, but the increase in mileage from Simandou should help shipping rates remain stable.

Fleet Breakeven Low, Utilization Rate High

The main advantage a company like Seanergy holds is its low fleet breakeven. This has largely remained the same since last quarter, with this quarter's data highlighting a slight uptick for daily vessel operating expenses from $7,222 (as reported in the Q3 2025 press release) to $7,376 daily.

To calculate the 'true' breakeven, I will need to divide the interest cost, sitting at $5.37 million for this quarter, by the number of ownership days in this quarter, which is 1,911.

This results in a daily interest expense of $2,814, which, if added to the daily operating expenses, gives us a breakeven rate of $10,190.

Additionally, we will need a provision for daily dry-docking expense, which will be taken from the company's 2024 amortization of deferred dry-docking costs ($4.2 million) from this filing and divided by the 2024 ownership days (6,518) to get the per-day provision.

This calculation gives us a daily dry docking provision of $645, which, if added to the previous subtotal of $10,190, we arrive at $10,835 per day. When comparing this to the previous quarter's breakeven cost of $10,761 per day, the total change is an increase of $74 or an increase of less than 1%, which is statistically irrelevant.

On the other hand, a more significant development is the increase in fleet utilization of 3.2 percentage points over the quarter. This is in line with expectations of lower off-hire days when compared to Q2 2025. Management was quoted as saying "expect around 90 to 110, 120 off-hire days for dry dockings in the second half." in the Q2 2025 earnings call, " Half of it is going to be in this quarter and around 60 to 70 days in the fourth quarter."

Due to a combination of a strong market during Q4 and what management has guided for off-hire days, we can expect fleet utilization to remain largely the same, with a conservative estimate of 95%, which allows room for error.

Q4 2025 TCE estimates

With Seanergy Maritime using index-linked charters on all of its ships, this grants them significant exposure to the ups and downs of the capesize market. For all intents and purposes, this functions the same as if a ship were on spot employment, except that most ships under this agreement also hold an FFA conversion option.

With the FFA conversion option being available to all but two ships as of the latest information and management seeing strength in rates, we have seen an announcement that 75% of all operating days have been fixed at $23,478 daily for Q4 2025.

Given that we averaged $26,916 per day according to this article in Q4 2025, this can help us come to an estimate for daily TCE revenues in Q4.

We do this by multiplying 23,478 by 0.75 (giving it a 75% weight) and then adding it to the multiplication of 26,916 by 0.25 (giving it a 25% weight).

The result ends up being $24,338 per day in TCE (rounded up to the nearest whole number). Naturally, this number can vary slightly, but expect the daily rate declared by Seanergy Maritime to be more or less around the figure I provided.

Capesize Supply to Shrink

Supply is quite a simple story, with an order book of 9.3%, according to CMB.TECH's recent earnings release. Below is an extract from their earnings release focusing on their view of the capesize+newcastlemax order book along with some commentary.

Additionally, we can expect real supply to actually shrink over time as 32% of all ships are over 15 years old and being pressured by environmental regulations, which may force slower sailing speeds and scrapping of some of the older, non-environmentally compliant tonnage.

Iron Ore's Importance

Moving onto demand, iron ore makes up most of all capesize cargoes, of which a majority heads for China, according to this article. Nevertheless, it must be noted that bauxite has seen significant growth and is also a tonne-mile-intensive trade, however, it has mostly been displacing decreasing coal trade rather than providing any meaningful replacement to iron, which makes up over 70% of all cargoes.

Due to iron's importance, I will focus my analysis on the fundamentals of iron ore, which mainly serves as an input for steel. Despite the Chinese property crisis continuing to weigh down overall domestic steel demand, other sectors have been on the rise, according to this article. Additionally, exports have served as a release valve for steel oversupply, however, this trend is likely to reverse following Beijing's move to regulate steel exports.

With steel demand in China experiencing headwinds, we cannot expect a scenario where iron ore imports skyrocket any time soon. We might even see a slight decrease in iron ore demand if we take into account other facts, such as port inventories of iron ore reaching a recent high.

Routes Determine Rates

Of even bigger importance to the demand picture is the source of the iron as cargo, as a longer trip results in better rates. The traditional routes are C3 (Brazil-China) and C5 (Australia-China) which cover approximately 25% and 55% of all cargoes, respectively.

It is predicted that a slow but steady increase in mileage is to come due to the start of Simandou's exports. What makes Simandou special is the quality of its iron ore, which will incentivize steel producers to begin importing from them. This will increase the miles that ships will need to transport iron ore from if some of the supply gets shifted from Australia.

Overall, I believe that the increase in mileage throughout 2026 and 2027 as the mine begins ramping up operations will be essential in keeping capesize rates attractive in the midst of a demand slump in steel.

Earnings Evaluation and Estimates

The most important aspect, of course, is evaluating if this is a worthwhile investment. To do this, I will attempt to estimate earnings for Q4 and give a rough estimate for 2026 and let you be the judge of whether this company is worth your capital.

Regarding Q4, we will be using the previously calculated daily revenue rate and subtracting the daily breakeven rate ($24,338-$10,835) and applying a 95% utilization rate on 20 ships.

The final calculation comes to a profit of $23.4 million. Naturally, we need to deduct SG&A, which I will keep at $5 million for simplicity. This gives us net cash flow of $17.4 million for Q4 2025, which I don't need to tell you is attractive for a $200 million valuation.

While 2026 is expected to be softer, I do believe the market is pricing for a disaster scenario. I must stress that current capesize rates remain above $20,000 per day despite the weak seasonality of Q1, and if rates remain above $20,000, which is a very probable scenario, we can expect a net profit of $58.5 per year, which is over a 20% yearly earnings yield.

Risks and Catalysts

Expect the main risk to be Chinese Iron Ore demand slipping more than expected due to unprofitable steel mills. Readers are advised to monitor the health of the Chinese economy, and should we see a recovery from the property crisis, this could spark a catalyst for rates to move upwards, pushing company earnings up with it.

Conclusion and Price Target

While this is a complex market to navigate, I do believe there is ample opportunity at the current market cap, which provides a valuable way to enter a potentially high-margin opportunity in shipping.

Given that, I rate this stock a strong buy and raise my price target to $14.50 a share or a market cap of approximately $300 million. This reflects my belief that rates will average $20,000 per day in 2026, which would grant a net income of $58.5 million for the year.

data.title: U.S. Equity REITs Trade At Median 15% Discount To Analyst Price Targets
data.description:
All 123 publicly traded US equity real estate investment trusts analyzed by S&P Global Market Intelligence traded below their respective consensus price target estimates.

The REITs had a median implied upside of 15.0% to their consensus S&P Global Market Intelligence price target estimates as of Jan. 5.

The analysis included US-listed equity REITs that traded on the Nasdaq, NYSE or NYSE American and had at least three S&P Global Market Intelligence price target estimates.

Specialty REITs trade with highest median implied upside

The specialty sector, which includes advertising, casinos, communications, data centers, energy infrastructure, farmland and timber, had the largest median implied upside to its consensus price target estimate, at 22.6%.

Within the sector, Fermi Inc. (FRMI) had the largest implied upside among all analyzed REITs as of Jan. 5, at over 200%. The REIT's share plunged mid-December 2025 after it revealed that a prospective tenant at its 11-GW data center campus in Texas had withdrawn from a lease agreement worth approximately $150 million.

Iron Mountain Inc. (IRM), a storage and information management REIT in the specialty sector, had an implied upside of 38.3% and ranked sixth.

Five more specialty REITs ranked among the top 20 for implied upside: communications-focused American Tower Corp. (AMT) at 28.3% and Crown Castle Inc. (CCI) at 26.7%, data center REIT Digital Realty Trust Inc. (DLR) at 26.6%, casino landlord VICI Properties Inc. (VICI) at 26.0% and data center-focused Equinix Inc. (EQIX) at 23.7%.

REITs in the self-storage segment showed a 20.6% median implied upside to their consensus price target estimates, while the office sector followed closely at 18.7% and the residential sector at 15.4%.

The hotel sector had the smallest implied upside, with a median implied upside of 9.2%.

US REITs generally underperformed the broader market over the past year. The Dow Jones Equity All REIT index recorded a one-year total return of 3.7% as of Jan. 5, which was much lower than the S&P 500's return of 17.0% over the same period.

REITs with the highest implied upside

Following Fermi, office REIT Hudson Pacific Properties Inc. (HPP) ranked second for implied upside to the price target estimate. As of the market close on Jan. 5, its shares were priced at $10.98, which is slightly more than half of the consensus price target estimate of $20.67. Shopping center-focused SITE Centers Corp. (SITC) ranked third, with a consensus price target estimate 60.0% above its share price.

REITs with the least implied upside

OUTFRONT Media Inc. (OUT), an advertising-focused specialty REIT, had the lowest implied upside to its price target estimate, closing at $24.08 per share on Jan. 5, near its consensus price target of $24.20. Healthcare REIT Medical Properties Trust Inc. (MPW) and communications REIT Uniti Group Inc. (UNIT) followed, with consensus price targets set at 1.5% and 1.8% above their respective share prices as of Jan. 5.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Beyond The S&P 500: From Concentration To Conviction
data.description:
By Christopher Gannatti, CFA

When investors talk about the S&P 500, they often imagine a broad reflection of the U.S. economy, but in reality, the Index's behavior is increasingly dictated by a small cluster of mega-cap firms. Today, just seven companies, NVIDIA, Microsoft, Apple, Alphabet, Amazon, Meta and Tesla, account for nearly 40% of its total market capitalization. This level of concentration rivals or exceeds any prior period in modern market history, amplifying both returns and risk. In essence, the S&P 500 has become less a diversified portfolio and more a proxy for the world's most dominant technology platforms.

Figure 1: Viewing the Concentration of Exposure in the Magnificent 7

Many investors that we speak to are thinking about ways to de-emphasize this concentration. A simple approach is to look at the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index. The weights in the Magnificent 7 here were:1

  • NVIDIA: 0.23%
  • Tesla: 0.25%
  • Apple: 0.24%
  • Amazon.com: 0.19%
  • Alphabet, across two share classes: 0.23%
  • Microsoft: 0.21%
  • Meta Platforms: 0.18%

These weights make sense in that weighting 500 stocks equally would anchor at 0.20% per stock on the date of rebalancing. However, treating 500 companies exactly the same by way of weighting may be too strong a reaction to concentration risk. People may not want the weight in seven stocks to approach 40%, but they may also recognize that some companies are stronger than others.

Performance, Where the Rubber Meets the Road

Under its current methodology,2 the WisdomTree U.S. Value Fund (WTV) has consistently outperformed the Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP)3 across multiple time horizons. WTV's fundamental weighting, focusing on companies reducing share counts and providing strong total shareholder yield, has proven more effective. By uniting diversification, valuation discipline and quality, WTV has turned its design into sustained outperformance.

Bottom Line: Both WTV and RSP sidestep the current concentration in very large companies that many see when they look at the S&P 500 Index. RSP is tracking a strategy that simply applies diversification by equalweighting; WTV, on the other hand, is focusing on a measure of investment fundamentals.

Figure 2a: How WTV Has Delivered beyond Equal Weight

Figure 2b: Standardized Performance

Step One: From Market Weight to Equal Weight

It is natural to want to remove concentration in a small subset of stocks. That's the premise of the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index, where each company holds roughly the same 0.2% weight. The result is a more balanced and democratic version of the S&P 500, but with trade-offs.

In figure 3, we see that quality, measured by return on equity (ROE), declines sharply. The share of firms earning more than 30% ROE falls from 43.6% to 22.4%, while exposure to sub-10% or negative ROE companies doubles. Equal weighting removes concentration but also dilutes quality, spreading exposure across strong and weak businesses alike.

Figure 4 shows the valuation shift: the Index becomes cheaper, with exposure to high P/E names (30x–35x) dropping from 30% to about 8% and weight in single-digit P/E stocks tripling. Yet that value improvement comes at the cost of the efficiency that made large firms dominant in the first place.

In short, the equal-weight approach broadens diversification, but at the expense of quality. The question remains: can we reduce concentration without abandoning excellence?

Step Two: From Equal Weight to WTV

WTV seeks to answer that question. Instead of equal weighting every company or following market cap, WTV weights primarily based on a fundamental concept: a company's ability to return cash to shareholders—in other words, the concept of total shareholder yield.

Buybacks, when done sustainably, are one of the clearest signals of capital discipline. They reflect management teams confident in their balance sheets and long-term prospects. By prioritizing firms that are consistently shrinking their share bases, WTV builds a portfolio that is both quality-conscious and valuation-aware.

Figure 3: The Hidden Quality Trade-off in Equal-Weight Indexing

Conclusion: Valuation Discipline Without Sacrificing Fundamentals

Turning to valuation, WTV pushes the improvement further than the Equal Weight Index, adding valuation discipline while preserving the quality profile seen earlier. As shown in figure 4, more than 50% of WTV's weight is in stocks trading below 15x earnings, compared to just 3% in the S&P 500. High-multiple exposure (above 30x) is nearly eliminated.

The result is valuation compression without quality loss: WTV owns companies that are cheap for good reasons, those using their earnings power to repurchase shares and reinvest productively, not firms that are merely inexpensive because they're weak.

Figure 4: How WTV Anchors Exposure in Fundamentally Priced Stocks

Figure 5: Additional Information

Footnotes

1 Sources: FactSet, S&P, with data as of 10/30/25.

2 WTV's current methodology has been in place since 12/18/17 and references the direct focus on total shareholder yield. Prior to that date, WTV was tracking a different investment strategy that was defined by the WisdomTree U.S. LargeCap Value Index.

3 RSP is designed to track the total return performance, before fees and expenses, of the S&P 500 Equal Weight Index.

Important Risks Related to this Article

There are risks associated with investing, including the possible loss of principal. Funds focusing their investments in certain sectors increase their vulnerability to any single economic or regulatory development. This may result in greater share price volatility. While the Fund is actively managed, the Fund’s investment process is expected to be heavily dependent on quantitative models, and the models may not perform as intended. Please read the Fund’s prospectus for specific details regarding the Fund’s risk profile.

Christopher Gannatti, CFA, Global Head of Research

Christopher Gannatti began at WisdomTree as a Research Analyst in December 2010, working directly with Jeremy Schwartz, CFA®, Director of Research. In January of 2014, he was promoted to Associate Director of Research where he was responsible to lead different groups of analysts and strategists within the broader Research team at WisdomTree. In February of 2018, Christopher was promoted to Head of Research, Europe, where he was based out of WisdomTree’s London office and was responsible for the full WisdomTree research effort within the European market, as well as supporting the UCITs platform globally. In November 2021, Christopher was promoted to Global Head of Research, now responsible for numerous communications on investment strategy globally, particularly in the thematic equity space. Christopher came to WisdomTree from Lord Abbett, where he worked for four and a half years as a Regional Consultant. He received his MBA in Quantitative Finance, Accounting, and Economics from NYU’s Stern School of Business in 2010, and he received his bachelor’s degree from Colgate University in Economics in 2006. Christopher is a holder of the Chartered Financial Analyst Designation.

Original Post

data.title: 2026 Credit Outlook: Growing Divergence Amid AI's Big Build-Out
data.description:
By William Smith | Timothy Kurpis, CFA | Robert Hopper

The credit markets could see increased dispersion in 2026—amplified by AI’s infrastructure build-out.

The credit markets begin 2026 on solid ground, buoyed by resilient corporate fundamentals, elevated yields and relatively low default rates. But investors face headwinds in tight credit spreads, and we expect increased bond-market dispersion—amplified by a large-scale build-out of artificial intelligence (AI) infrastructure. In our view, picking tomorrow’s winners in this evolving landscape will make credit selection more important than ever.

The AI Building Boom Has Begun

AI’s golden age has yet to arrive, but the expansion phase has already begun, with myriad implications for fixed-income investors. Hyperscalers—large cloud-service and infrastructure providers—are in a race to fund training superclusters and globally distributed inference capacity. The stakes—and the cash burn—are enormous. Fortunately, these technology behemoths have robust cash flows and strong balance sheets that make it possible to tap the investment-grade bond market for a portion of this funding. Hyperscalers also fill a need for higher-quality fixed-income assets, helping to balance supply with demand.

As infrastructure providers ramp up spending on data centers, energy infrastructure and networks, we expect to see investment-grade supply expand significantly in 2026—albeit at manageable levels within the public markets. Consider that the five major hyperscalers—Amazon, Alphabet Inc., Apple, Meta Platforms and Microsoft—comprise nearly 20% of the broader equity market but only 3.5% of public investment-grade debt. That’s due not only to the scale of the investment-grade bond universe but also to the growing role of private credit in offering niche funding solutions that the public markets can’t—or won’t—provide. Moreover, some AI-adjacent expenditures in power generation and grid connectivity are occurring outside the domain of hyperscalers.

We view credit growth tied to AI, particularly in power and grid investments, as both an opportunity and a potential source of friction. With private credit shouldering more of the funding load, we don’t expect the public credit markets to be swamped with new issuance over the coming year. That should reduce the burden on the public investment-grade market.

Still, risks remain in the form of overbuilding, uncertain demand and stranded assets. Fortunately, these haven’t escaped the market’s watchful eye. Large-cap tech issuers have lagged the broader investment-grade market modestly in recent months as investors scrutinize the AI build-out. In our view, that’s not stress; it’s the market beginning to price in uncertainty, which we view as healthy.

Dispersion Could Take Many Forms

We see 2026 as a divergence year marked by softening corporate fundamentals and higher dispersion, which could be amplified by AI. The investment-grade universe—increasingly populated by well-capitalized hyperscalers—has seen many more upgrades than downgrades in recent years (Display).

The Investment-Grade Market Has Recently Seen More Upgrades than Downgrades

Bloomberg US Corporate Index: Ratings Changes

Conversely, weakening fundamentals may be felt in more levered capital structures, including single-B and CCC-rated cohorts. Across the rest of the high-yield universe, we’re cautious about possible changes to leverage policies for capital allocation purposes or for more aggressive M&A activity. In our view, the market isn’t pricing this risk appropriately, making higher-rated credit more attractive to us. We think the sweet spot in 2026 will center on BBBs and BBs—a crossover zone between investment grade and high yield.

Owing to elevated yields and much lower default risk, BB-rated bonds were steady performers over the past year—a trend we expect to continue in 2026. For investors wary of high yield, BBBs may be an appealing surrogate. In fact, many BBBs currently offer BB-like yields or better, putting investors in the enviable position of getting compensated more for assuming less risk—particularly in the US (Display).

With Spread Differentials Falling, BBBs Look Attractive

Relative Spread: BB vs. BBB (Basis Points)

However, we expect to see growing dispersion even within BBBs, so investors should focus on credits with downgrade risk already baked into valuations. Our credit research suggests relatively limited fallen-angel risk at the index level. But market pricing implies a more cautious view, with a noticeable number of securities trading like downgrade candidates.

Comfort in the Belly (of the Curve)

AI-linked capex also carries yield-curve implications. Large global technology platforms issued more than $100 billion in investment-grade bonds in 2025, with roughly half of that issuance in maturities longer than 10 years. Given current valuations, we’re concerned about the effects this supply surge could have on longer-term investment-grade spreads, as well as the effect of higher US deficit spending on longer-dated US Treasuries.

While recent steepening in the US Treasury yield curve might tempt investors to extend longer, we believe intermediate-term maturities deserve a closer look. In our analysis, the belly of the curve looks attractive on three fronts—relative value, yields and spreads (Display). Simply put, this is where we believe investors will be best compensated for risk.

Yield and Spread Compensation Are Attractive in the Belly of the Curve

Yield and Spread Difference Between Long and Intermediate Maturities

At first blush, the fly in the ointment is tight credit spreads, which could create headwinds to credit returns. But we don’t believe narrow spreads should deter investors from owning credit. A good share of the tightness is in the long end of the curve, and spreads tend to stay range-bound once they tighten (Display). Moreover, across investment grade and high yield, yields remain historically elevated, which should provide a cushion against rate volatility, with existing coupon income doing much of the heavy lifting.

Tight Spreads Can Persist for Long Periods

Bloomberg Global Aggregate Index: Spread (Basis Points)

The market’s animal spirits could also play a role over the coming year. Potential deregulation and an uptick in M&A activity could portend risks, but M&A is starting from a low baseline, and we still view the current funding mix as creditor-friendly.

Hyperscalers are creating what appears to be a durable supply pipeline, but it’s too early to deem AI a smashing success or a bubble about to burst. The reality on the ground is more nuanced, with both opportunities and concerns. Fortunately, parts of the market already reflect these concerns, and we expect bouts of volatility in 2026 to create attractive entry points for investors who can lean into those dislocations. With the emerging AI epoch still evolving, we expect the coming year to require patience—and thoughtful credit selection—until AI’s potential is more fully realized.

The views expressed herein do not constitute research, investment advice or trade recommendations, do not necessarily represent the views of all AB portfolio-management teams and are subject to change over time.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Whale's Tradingview: How To Trade In 2026? (Part 1)
data.description:
Bretton Woods III: From the US Dollar to Commodities

Silver has emerged as the latest standout across global markets. On the first trading day of the year, the price rose 10% intraday; over the past six months, it has advanced by more than 105%. With spot silver briefly trading above $80, recent price action is exceptional by historical standards.

Source: Tradingview

The rally is not confined to silver. Precious, base and industrial metals have broadly repriced higher: copper is up 37.23% over the past year, gold has gained 67.73%, and lithium carbonate has increased by more than 100%.

While short squeezes have contributed marginally, a sustained uptrend extending beyond a year is unlikely to be explained solely by positioning dynamics.

Since around 2022, a set of deeper, structurally persistent forces has begun to reshape the macro-financial regime for years - potentially decades - ahead. The re-rating in metals should be viewed as one expression of that regime shift.

Source: Tradingview

Source: Tradingview

For much of the post-war period, global investors have treated the US dollar as the default unit of account and settlement currency. A Federal Reserve report published in July 2025 notes that the dollar still represents close to 50% of international trade invoicing and remains dominant in FX trading; US Treasuries also continue to function as a widely accepted form of collateral.

Source: federalreserve.gov

However, the risk framework has changed. The increasing “weaponisation” of the dollar and US Treasuries, together with heightened uncertainty around US monetary policy and domestic politics, has forced investors to reassess tail risks.

In particular, the prospect of frozen USD deposits and/or challenged settlement on Treasury holdings under broad compliance or national security pretexts has become more salient. Under these conditions, incremental diversification away from the dollar is a rational portfolio response.

Zoltan Pozsar (formerly head of short-term rates strategy at Credit Suisse) has characterised this transition as “Bretton Woods III.” In a world where the dollar is not fully trusted - even if it retains superior liquidity - portfolio optimisation tends to increase allocations to non-USD currencies, quasi-monetary assets, and non-USD-denominated risk assets.

Relative equity performance is consistent with this dynamic: in 2025, MSCI Europe returned 36.3% and MSCI Emerging Markets gained 34.4%, versus 17.9% for the S&P 500. A euro appreciation of more than 10% against the dollar over the past year further widened the return differential between European and US equities in USD terms.

Source: Creative Planning

Commodities, long regarded as a form of “quasi-money,” are a natural beneficiary of this shift. Policymakers can influence the dollar and the Treasury market; they cannot directly control the supply-demand balance of metals.

A similar logic applies to other exchangeable commodities (e.g., crude oil, timber): real assets can be bartered indirectly through global trade linkages, even when settlement currency preferences evolve.

Fiat currencies will continue to purchase commodities; however, in a regime dominated by national security and risk diversification, commodity prices are likely to embed a material risk premium - reflecting potential sanctions, tariffs, supply chain uncertainty and additional freight/insurance costs.

A further tailwind could emerge from a potential change in the policy reaction function at the Federal Reserve in 2026. If rate decisions become more explicitly politicised, inflation expectations may become less anchored.

The transmission mechanism is straightforward: policy easing that is inconsistent with underlying inflation dynamics can shift corporate and investor behaviour towards worst-case planning, reinforcing price-setting power through the supply chain. Once downstream participants accept higher prices on the basis of elevated inflation expectations, higher USD commodity prices become increasingly difficult to avoid.

How to Trade?

Cash and currency allocation

With the Fed still in an easing cycle, liquidity support having resumed (albeit in a measured fashion aimed at maintaining market functioning), and institutional/political risks rising into 2026, a high USD cash allocation appears less compelling on a risk-adjusted basis. Viable alternatives include:

Source: CME group

  • EUR: Relatively predictable policy setting; remains the second most important international currency.

  • CHF: A traditional safe-haven allocation in risk-off environments.

  • AUD: Structurally levered to a commodity upcycle.

A practical implementation is to reduce USD cash weight and increase exposure to the above currencies within a diversified liquidity sleeve.

Metals exposure via “spot + options”

Given expectations for continued strength in metals, a long allocation to metals ETFs (e.g., SLV) is directionally consistent. However, with higher participation and greater speculative activity, realised and implied volatility are likely to remain elevated. SLV 30-day implied volatility is approximately 63.77%, compared with roughly 43% for BTC over the same horizon.

Source: optioncharts.io

Against this backdrop, an option overlay can improve outcomes. Current precious metals ETF option skews are tilted towards the call side (calls relatively expensive vs. puts), creating an opportunity to monetise asymmetry:

  • Long spot/ETF

  • Sell quarterly calls

  • Buy quarterly puts

Expected profile:

  • Upside scenario: The payoff increasingly resembles a call-spread-like return profile; gains can be crystallised, and the structure rolled as spot advances.

  • Downside scenario: The put provides convex protection; if a downtrend is confirmed, the spot leg can be reduced, while the hedge may generate an outsized profit and loss (P&L).

  • Range-bound scenario: Normalisation of skew and time decay dynamics can contribute incremental carry.

Equities and crypto positioning for 2026 will be addressed in Part 2. Stay tuned!

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rocket Lab: Innovative Company, But Very Expensive Stock
data.description:
Editor's note: Seeking Alpha is proud to welcome David B McMillan as a new contributing analyst. You can become one too! Share your best investment idea by submitting your article for review to our editors. Get published, earn money, and unlock exclusive SA Premium access. Click here to find out more »

Overview

In this article I examine Rocket Lab's (RKLB) current valuation and whether it can really be justified by fundamentals, when accounting for risks of failure. My thesis: The market has a history of overhyping and overvaluing cool design prototypes before they've been tested by physics and economics. RKLB is priced for perfect execution, while the risks make that outcome far from certain.

Introduction - Rocket Lab's Stock Surge

Rocket Lab's stock absolutely surged in 2025, ending the year up an eye-watering 188%. In the past five years, the stock is up nearly 700% to a market capitalization of about $44 billion at the time of writing. Rocket Lab, however, is not the first company to see its stock take off even faster than its rockets. From December 2019 to February 2021, Virgin Galactic’s stock shot up by 650%, taking the company’s market cap to $24 billion. Since then, SPCE has deteriorated, losing 99.7% of its value. The company is now worth only $211 million.

While Rocket Lab is certainly a different company than Virgin Galactic, with a much more promising business plan, the extreme valuations that SPCE reached in 2021 on a prototype rocket with minimal total addressable market should serve as a warning to investors buying into space-related companies. The market is prone to overvaluing cool-but-untested prototypes, with business reality taking the backseat. The reality is that Rocket Lab is in direct competition with one of the most innovative companies that has ever existed and which is likely to IPO in 2026 - SpaceX. Whether Rocket Lab is the next Virgin Galactic or the next SpaceX will hinge on the proposed economics of its launch system, requiring a deep dig into the numbers to understand.

Business Overview - Rocket Lab's Product Lines

Rocket Lab currently has one operational rocket - the Electron rocket - with another - the Neutron rocket - promised to begin test launches in 2026. Let’s look at what each rocket can do:

Electron Rocket

  • Can deliver 300 kg payload to low earth orbit (LEO)

  • Cost of $7.5 million per launch

  • 21 launches in 2025 (100% success rate)

In total, the Electron rocket has launched successfully 68 times through Sept 2025. The COGS per flight is approximately $4.2-4.5 million (resulting in about $3-$3.3 million in gross profit per launch).

If we do some back-of-the-napkin math, how many launches would it take for an investor to recoup a $44 billion investment at $3 million gross profit per launch? About 14,667 launches. If I wanted to double my investment in 5 years, it would take 2933 launches per year, or 8 launches every day, and that’s if we start the clock right now. Those numbers ignore SG&A expenses, which eat up about 80% of gross profit currently, and R&D expenses, which alone exceed gross profit. (Note that this article does not contain a full DCF valuation analysis - "recouping investment" is just an illustrative heuristic).

So clearly launches of the Electron rocket alone can’t justify the company’s price tag. Rocket Lab is addressing that by also developing the Neutron rocket, which is designed to carry a much larger payload, and so-called “Space Systems”, which the company defines as the “design and manufacture of spacecraft components and spacecraft program management services, space data applications, mission operations, and optical systems”. Let’s first look at the planned capabilities of the Neutron rocket:

Neutron Rocket

  • Capacity to low earth orbit of 15,000 kg in “fully expendable” mode

  • 13,000 kg capability if landing on a sea barge, 8,000 kg if returning to the launch pad

  • Target price of $50 million per launch, with a gross margin similar to Electron

The Neutron rocket is much larger than the Electron, and is designed to deploy between 10 and 50 satellites to LEO. However, the rocket has a number of critical risk factors in its design. It famously uses a hungry hippo fairing, but this design is both revolutionary and risky. The nose cone of the rocket is precisely the part on which there is the greatest aerodynamic pressure during flight. Rocket Lab is proposing to create a mechanical system on this part of the rocket, introducing hinges, seals, and actuation mechanisms exactly where the vehicle experiences the most intense physical forces. Although the company is testing the system in simulations, real life tends to contain unforeseen challenges.

Further complicating the design is the heavy reliance on carbon fiber in the ship's design. The rocket's mold is created by Rocket Lab's remarkable Automated Fiber Placement machine (AFP machine), which essentially 3D prints a large part of the rocket's body and components. If it works, carbon fiber as a material of rocketry makes a lot of sense: it has an excellent strength-to-weight ratio, which means a lighter, more efficient rocket, and is cheaper than the alternatives. But it's also incredibly risky and untested - SpaceX itself moved away from carbon fiber tanks primarily because of the difficulty they present for reusability. The material is brittle and fails suddenly, unlike steel, which is malleable and bends before it breaks. The material also hides damage - it's not easy to inspect and repair between flights. Rocket Lab doesn't have SpaceX's capital buffer, which means it doesn't have the luxury of blowing up vehicle after vehicle in testing. The Neutron rocket needs to thread a needle, with multiple revolutionary design aspects that all need to be successful before the company burns through its cash pile.

So if Neutron makes $25 million in gross profit per launch, how many launches do we need in 5 years to recoup our $44 billion investment? We would still need 1760 launches - a cadence of nearly 1 launch per day for a rocket that hasn’t even been tested yet. Still unviable economics. So do 'Space Systems' get us to economic viability? This is where the story starts to get interesting.

Space Systems

  • Photon satellites: typical price of $2-6 million each, with a gross margin of about 37%

  • Lightning satellites: larger with a planned price of $25-50 million each

Rocket Lab has recently secured a US Space Development Agency contract worth $816 million to design and manufacture 18 satellites for the US Government. CEO Peter Beck has emphasized the importance of this "Space Systems" segment, repeatedly making the case that the company can stand out from the competition through vertical integration, as a "one stop shop" for combined launch and satellite manufacturing services.

So do Space Systems make the valuation math work? If Rocket Lab can make a gross profit of $250 million per launch (by charging for rocket launches and satellites buses as a combined package), then it would take just 176 launches to "recoup" the $44 billion cost of the company - far more plausible. But Rocket Lab is a long way from making that vision a reality, and everything has to go according to plan. The Neutron rocket has to work as intended and become commercialized, and soon. Satellite production needs to be scaled up, which is not a straightforward task. 85 launches each year of 15 satellites would require manufacturing 1275 satellites in a calendar year. Lockheed Martin (LMT) is one of the dominant players in mid-sized satellite production and they produce about 180 satellites per year. SpaceX itself creates thousands of satellites per year for Starlink, but those satellites are small - Rocket Lab's proposition to mass produce Lightning satellite buses is a higher bar. If Rocket Lab were to hit Lockheed's satellite production cadence and could launch those satellites on Neutron, it could yield about $3 billion per year in revenue, which still makes the current $44 billion valuation look very expensive. And we're still likely years away from that kind of launch cadence being a reality.

Rocket Lab's Value Proposition & Where It Could Break

Clearly the story behind Rocket Lab’s valuation is something like this: we’ll manufacture your satellites in house and then launch them on our own rocket into a private constellation. But there are a lot of “ifs” in that story that could break the entire chain.

Why It's Risky (Rocket Science is Hard)

Everything about Rocket Lab’s value proposition depends on the success of the Neutron rocket. A Neutron failure would break the “vertically integrated space company” narrative. Neutron has to both work and be scalable, which may be a tougher feat than investors appreciate. Already, the launch of Neutron has been delayed several times. If everything goes according to plan, investors in Rocket Lab may be handsomely rewarded. However, that is far from a certainty, and the market has a worrying history of systematically underestimating the difficulty of rocket science.

Other Bullish Arguments (And Why They Miss the Mark)

Those bullish on RKLB would point out that government contracts could be a major financial boon to the company. It is true that the US government does not want space services to be monopolized by SpaceX, so there is incentive to throw public money at SpaceX competitors. But that funding may also dry up if the Neutron rocket does not succeed quickly.

Another area of opportunity cited by bulls relates to the Golden Dome missile defense project. CEO Peter Beck leaned heavily into this in the most recent earnings call, saying that Rocket Lab's leanness is necessary to "meet the administration's urgent timeline" and that "we stand ready to deliver for the Golden Dome". The Golden Dome project is an opportunity for the company since it would require sending an enormous amount of payload to orbit. However, it's very possible that the project gets significantly scaled back on cost concerns. Because it is cheap for adversaries to overwhelm a defense system with offensive weapons, the economics tend to favor offensive, rather than defensive, deterrence. The Trump Administration only has three years left in office, which does not leave much time if a new administration in 2029 decides that the benefits aren't worth the costs. If the market is pricing potential Golden Dome revenue into RKLB's valuation, then a scaling back or abandonment of the project could be a negative catalyst for the stock.

Conclusion

So to recap: I believe Rocket Lab is an exciting company, but for me the stock is overvalued on an unproven promise. Just about everything has to go right for the risk to justify the reward: Neutron needs to prove itself capable of both delivery to LEO and scalability in the near future - a failure of the rocket on the launchpad could cause the stock to crater in my view. There needs to be a sizable market for the 'Space Systems' segment and there has to be sufficient demand for an alternative to SpaceX even if that alternative is more expensive. A SpaceX IPO in 2026 could hurt the stock as well, because it gives retail investors an alternative public company to invest in if they want to be invested in space commercialization.

While I certainly wouldn't bet against this company, the stock is trading at too high a premium for me to invest at the current moment.

data.title: Visible Alpha Breakdown Of U.S. Banks' Fourth Quarter Earnings Expectations
data.description:
US big banks kick off fourth quarter earnings season on Tuesday, January 13, with JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) reporting first, followed by Bank of America Corp. (BAC), Citigroup Inc. (C), and Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) on Wednesday. Based on Visible Alpha consensus estimates, the big banks are expected to end fiscal 2025 on a relatively strong footing, building on resilient earnings through the first three quarters of the year.

All four of the large US banks are expected to deliver mid-single- to high-single-digit year-on-year revenue growth, reflecting resilient fee income and stable net interest income (NII). Bank of America is seen leading the earnings momentum, with net income projected to rise nearly 13% year-on-year, supported by strong operating leverage and balance sheet growth. Wells Fargo also shows healthy earnings growth, with net income expected to grow 8.7% year-on-year, driven by improved efficiency and higher NII.

In contrast, JPMorgan and Citi present a more subdued earnings picture. JPMorgan’s net income growth is expected to be modest (+0.5% YOY), suggesting margin pressure and elevated costs offsetting revenue gains. Citi’s earnings are expected to be slightly negative, driven by ongoing profitability challenges amid restructuring efforts.

All four banks have announced sizeable share buybacks in 2025, helping lift EPS growth above the pace of net income expansion.

Credit quality is expected to remain broadly stable across the four banks, with non-performing assets (NPAs) holding below 1% of total loans for all major lenders. However, most banks are building reserves in response to slight quarter-on-quarter NPA increases. Capital ratios remain comfortably above regulatory minimums. JPMorgan and Bank of America are expected to see slight quarter-on-quarter declines, reflecting balance sheet expansion and capital deployment, while Citi posts a modest improvement, aligning with its capital optimization efforts.

Profitability and efficiency:

Analysts expect NIMs to improve modestly in Q4 2025, averaging 2.40% across the four banks, up from 2.37% in the prior quarter. NII trends remain constructive, supported by balance sheet expansion and higher average earning assets. Aggregate NII is expected to rise nearly 7% year-on-year in Q4 2025, with Bank of America and Citi projected to deliver the strongest NII growth (both above 8% YOY), reflecting loan growth and international contributions.

However, NII momentum is expected to moderate into Q1 2026, signaling a more normalized earnings environment as rate tailwinds fade.

Efficiency trends highlight increasing divergence in operating leverage across the large banks. Wells Fargo is expected to see the clearest structural improvement, with its efficiency ratio declining steadily from 68% in Q4 2024 to 62.9% in Q4 2025, reflecting cost discipline and operational streamlining.

Balance sheet and scale:

All four major banks show healthy asset growth into Q4 2025, reflecting both organic balance sheet expansion and exposure to strong market activity. JPMorgan leads in scale, with total assets approaching $4.6 trillion in Q4 (+14.3% YOY), supported by a combination of lending growth, robust trading, and investment banking activity. Citi is also expected to see strong asset growth (+12.6% YOY), reflecting portfolio expansion, especially in international markets, and ongoing investment banking activity.

On the deposits front, analysts expect steady growth, supporting lending capacity and NII. A faster pace of monetary easing by the Fed could further lift growth in 2026. Meanwhile, loan portfolios are projected to expand across all banks, with the group’s Q4 net loans totaling $4.3 trillion. Loan growth could accelerate further if interest rates decline and the economic outlook remains stable.

Credit quality:

Credit quality is expected to remain broadly stable in Q4 2025. Aggregate provisions for loan losses are expected to total $8.3 billion in Q4, up 6.3% year-on-year, signaling a cautious stance despite a generally healthy credit environment. NPAs are expected to remain low across the banks, averaging 0.65% in Q4, indicating strong underlying credit quality.

Capital levels remain robust, with the common equity tier 1 (CET1) ratio expected to remain above regulatory minimums across the group, averaging 13% in Q4, providing strong buffers for lending and shareholder returns.

Overall, US big banks are expected to end 2025 with solid balance sheets, stable credit quality, and selective earnings momentum, positioning the group to navigate a more normalized rate environment in 2026
